title: Chicken biryani
subtitle: Layered spiced rice and chicken, steamed together
excerpt: Marinated chicken pieces and separately cooked saffron-tinted basmati rice layered in a pot, sealed, and finished over gentle heat so the rice absorbs the chicken's juices and spices from below while steaming from the residual heat above. A celebratory dish from the Mughal tradition, made distinctive by the dum technique where the sealed pot finishes in its own steam.
sourceNotes: Biryani is a Mughal court dish, brought to the Indian subcontinent from Persia. The dum method (sealed pot cooking) is integral to the technique. Chicken biryani is the most widely ordered biryani on the British Indian menu, documented in restaurant literature from the 1970s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the chicken the night before. Cook the day of serving.
recipe.temperatureNote: Oven temperature for the dum stage

content.text: Biryani is a two-component dish assembled in layers. The chicken must be cooked separately in its marinade until almost done before it goes into the pot. The rice must be parboiled separately to about 70% cooked (no more) so it finishes in the dum stage without becoming mushy. Both elements must be at the right stage simultaneously; that is the main coordination challenge.

The dum stage (sealed cooking in the oven) is not optional. The tight seal (foil under the lid) traps steam inside the pot, which finishes the rice while allowing the chicken juices to permeate upward. If the seal leaks, the rice dries out. Use a heavy casserole dish with a well-fitting lid and seal with foil if there is any doubt about the fit.

items.prepNote: soaked in 3 tbsp warm milk for 15 minutes
thinly sliced, for crispy fried onions

content.text: Marinate the chicken and fry the onions

Combine the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} with the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{chilli-powder}}, {{garam-masala}}, {{turmeric}}, and 1 teaspoon of salt. Mix well and marinate for at least 30 minutes or overnight. Fry the sliced {{onion}} in the {{vegetable-oil}} over medium-high heat for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring, until deep golden and crisp. Remove and drain on paper. Reserve the oil in the pan.
Cook the chicken and parboil the rice

Preheat the oven to 150°C. Cook the marinated chicken in the reserved oil over medium-high heat for 15 minutes, stirring, until the marinade has reduced and the chicken is nearly cooked through. Meanwhile, bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil with the {{cardamom-green}}, {{cinnamon-stick}}, and {{cloves}}. Add the drained {{basmati-rice}} and boil for 5 minutes — the rice should be about 70% cooked: tender on the outside but with a chalky core. Drain immediately.

In a heavy casserole or deep oven dish, spread the cooked chicken as the base layer. Spread half the rice over the chicken. Scatter half the fried onions over the rice. Drizzle half the saffron milk and half the {{ghee}} over the top. Add the remaining rice, remaining fried onions, remaining saffron milk, and remaining ghee. Cover with foil, then the lid. Bake in the oven for 25 minutes. Remove from the oven and rest, still sealed, for 10 minutes. Lift the foil at the table to serve.

items.cause: Parboiled too long before the dum stage
items.fix: The rice must be undercooked at the parboiling stage — it will finish in the oven. If parboiled to fully tender, reduce dum time to 15 minutes

items.symptom: Bottom of biryani is dry and the rice sticks
items.cause: Insufficient ghee or chicken marinade dried out
items.fix: Add an extra tablespoon of ghee to the chicken layer before adding the rice. A thin layer of moisture at the base steams upward through the rice

items.symptom: No saffron colour in the rice
items.cause: Saffron not soaked long enough or placed unevenly
items.fix: Soak the saffron in warm (not boiling) milk for at least 15 minutes. Drizzle in lines over the rice to create the characteristic yellow-and-white pattern
The chicken must be nearly cooked before the dum stage — it is finishing in the oven, not cooking for the first time

content.text: Biryani occupies a different position on the British curry-house menu from other rice dishes: it is served as a complete meal (chicken and rice together) rather than as a side. The layering and dum technique give it a visual drama when lifted at the table that pilau rice cannot match. In the curry-house tradition it is often served with raita on the side. Across South Asia, biryani styles vary enormously by region — Hyderabadi, Lucknowi, Kolkata, and Sindhi versions each have distinct characters; the British curry-house version draws loosely from the Mughal northern tradition.
